speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Mai Lu and Marie Lee Lu took place at their home in Moscow on January 20th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was just shocked to see it. I even read a government report on immigration, which was racist. It was terrible. It was just disgusting to read. When you started telling me that there was a law and that people couldn't come and that wives couldn't come, I started seeing that it was bad.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I just read an article in the paper about immigrants. This is Centennial Year and they say so many immigrants from Europe, so many immigrants from Asia, so many immigrants from China and Asian countries. And very little, less than that from Japan and less than that from India, has very little, less than 1%. They give a deal in millions. Just the other day I read that article in the, I forgot what it is. The magazine. Sunday magazine. Sunday magazine, which was telling the truth. Now that's for all the years now since the United States, like you say, 1776 or even before. I don't think there were any Chinese before that. It was only in the 1800s. When people were over here in the West, they needed labor and all that before they allowed them to even come in for that. One thing I was surprised to read about was how hostile the American labor unions were in the 1800s as seeing Chinese labor as competition. Not all, but most, they were right in the forefront of trying to keep people out. They spent a lot of money in Washington, D .C. too, you know, put up these laws to force them out. Well, the biggest revolution in allowing Chinese immigrants to come in, in allowing the Chinese people to become citizens, was at the time when Pearl Buck was, she was one of the leaders in pushing through that law. Well, if she was a leader in the war, and the Japanese war, the war combined. That was before the Japanese war, 1943, when the law became... During the war. Yeah, but in 1945 before Honolulu. No, no, it was 1940, you were one. Oh, oh, oh. Yeah, December... In 77, oh, that was in 71. I was thinking that, well, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much was life in smaller towns like Walla Walla, where you grew up, how much was that like the life in the cities, bigger cities, where they had bigger Chinatowns like San Francisco or Seattle? Do you think that they were very similar for the people in both places, or did it make a difference where they were?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I don't think it makes that much... You see, San Francisco has a bigger Chinatown in there, there's no doubt about that. San Francisco, so therefore the people in California are more...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: There are a lot of Spanish people in there too, and Italians, see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So I think they're better in California than they were... And Walla Walla wasn't too bad, because Walla Walla, a year ago we had a Calvary unit there. Fort Walla Walla, the Chinese were there when the fort was there, the first one they brought to the river was there. They used to have a fort in Walla Walla right south, I'd say west of town.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: One acre, you said?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, one mile square. Oh, one mile square. One mile square, yeah, right there. Was where the fort was. Where the fort was, yeah. And the colonels and those guys in the fort, they liked Chinese labor because the Chinese go cook for them, and they do all the odd jobs for them. So they protect the Chinese quite a bit in Walla Walla. In order to keep their... Yeah, the people didn't bother them too much.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: There are a few radical ones that did go and beat them up and all that stuff, you see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And the throw rocks at their windows and all that stuff.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: But as a general rule, they had one time they were trying to drive the Chinese out, but I think I heard the older Chinese said that the colonel put an order down and sent and helped the Chinese out because they didn't want to let them do that, see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Because they were really against the law, so they won't do it until the union backed out. The radical guys backed out. Maybe they could punish the higher -ups.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They could be punished when they do things like that, see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And then that would stop some of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the fort still there when you were a kid?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: There wasn't any cavalry there when I was a kid, but it wasn't too long ago when my dad was there. They used to be a fort there, Fort Walla Walla. Walla Walla is one of the oldest towns in this whole territory. That's when Marcus Whitman first came in there. I don't suppose it was too much longer after the first white settlers, the Chinese in there. They're right in there because I remember this, it must be 30 or 40 years ago, they had a walla walla called Centennial or some kind of a 100 or 150 year. They had a play pageant out in the fairgrounds. They showed where Fort Walla Walla was, in a little town where each store was. There were the Chinese people there with a little laundry running up and down the street with all the rest of them. That was a pageant. I don't recall it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the Chinese community had been there continuously from soon after when the fort came?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was a Chinese, what was the early ones there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, as far as I'm concerned, my father had a chance to buy that right in the center of town. Walla Walla is on Second Street and Main Street where the Baker -Borer Bank is right there, that corner for $600. So it must be quite a while ago before you can do that. That's right in the center of town where the bank is. At that time, when I was in Walla Walla at that time, the street was still muddy. There were not too many, very few. We had horses all over the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Horses and bikers?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Even taxis were horses. They had a carriage, two horses. It was quite nice. Even fire trucks used to be owned by horses. Oh yeah, fire trucks. But it was quite nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the Chinatown, you said it was about a block long there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: About half a block long.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I would say about 120 feet deep. It's a quarter of a block. About a quarter of a block.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they small houses?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no. This is Chinatown here before that. Chinatown was scattered all over Walla Walla. It was scattered from two or three blocks around there. But I think in 1911, the Chinese people got together and bought this little piece of land there and built this building there. Then they had these little stores of six, eight, eight, and I believe there's ten or twelve stores there. I think all together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it all one building?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: All one building.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Upstairs they had rooming for the people that come through town. The Chinese people that come through lived there. Then they had apartments up there. A few apartments that the rich Chinese had with their family. They had one or two apartments there. But most of these stores are for after the railroad was built and after the Chinese went into the truck gardening quite a bit. So each one of these stores here, like the Joneses and the Smiths and the Family, we're going to go and get a little store here. We buy and ship the food in. We buy from all the Joneses. We buy from that. This thing here, we finance the garden with the food all year until the crop comes. They sell the food to the garden. They sell the food to the garden. They mark it down. By the credit. Credit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many different families had stores in Luang Luang?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, there's a lot of families. There's a lot of all kinds. Long Yu Ma. Yeah. The Ainsley Cat one. Yeah. And the Long Lu Jiang. Yeah. You guys got one. And then there must be a Yu Wong. The other Lao Li Lu. Yeah, Lao Li Lu. Yeah, everybody. I don't remember Lao Li. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Li Po Lo. Oh, Li Po Lo. One of the, in some place I read that in the small and the Rocky Mountain towns and the smaller ones that there was only maybe four families that would have stores in one town. Yeah. So I was wondering how many different ones. That's just about all. See the Ainsley, the Lees, the Lu's and the Chams together, you know, because they're the four families that are the smaller ones. Uh huh. The Lees and the Wongs.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You know how many?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You know how many?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You say they're eight or nine?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You see those that haven't got that, their family is so small, they've got one or two living, they go into the friends. They go into the store and create with a friend. Yeah, that's what it is. And then don't forget most of these stores are food stores.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Now how about that? Do anybody ever run any kind of like country store with dish towels and a few underwears and stuff like that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah. I don't remember seeing a store like that. It's clothing. Clothing, you get overhauls there, you get shoes there. No, I think that kind of a store is almost like a department store. Well it is like a little bit of a department store. Maybe run by more than one family, maybe a Wong, no, Wong and Noing. No, they don't handle everything but they handle a few. Chinese people like to wear Chinese shoes, you know, those old Chinese slippers kind of like, you know. Yeah, and scrubs. They've got some what they call scrubs and Chinese slippers. It's a soft material slippers with leather here, soles.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You mean like a hai?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah, a hai. They ship all those in. We call that hai, you know, they ship those in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about work clothes?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They do once in a while they have overhauls. Overhauls. See at that time in Walla Walla there wasn't too many stores in the main part of Walla Walla. See at that time you see that a lot of the Chinese worked in the Chinese garden and don't speak English. So there naturally they go and buy from the stores there. Chinese people want English to be Chinese. Chinese people want English to be Chinese. So really it's a combination really, you know, and they do. And also they give them credit for the whole year and that's a help too, you know. When a family is poor, got to start with, got to eat today anyway, you know. And they don't collect the well. Day by day maybe they get a little bit, you know, but not very much. But maybe at the end of the year they get the biggest crops, you know. In the fall they harvest the big crops and stuff. This building was still there until about five years ago. Until they tore it down. It was five years ago. Because at that time, you know, the head men call us up to see if we're interested in retaining that, you know. We and a few other ones that could do it. My son was too far from Moscow and we don't want to have too much to do with him besides, you know, if we take it over or we form two or three friends to take it over. Then some of the other families say, boy, those guys really got a bargain, you know. Well, so we just let that go by, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there still head men left in the community?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes. Yes, they kind of looked at that. You know, that building was kind of a, you know, everybody donated some money. It's just kind of a corporation, except it didn't written down like a corporation. But when they built the building they needed some money so your worker there would say, okay, I don't need, I mean, not donate. They give it back to you. I'll lend you the hundred dollars over there. Then they charge rent and they pay you back with interest, you see. If things do good then you get more. If things don't do so good then you might have to lose a little bit of your, of the way you call it, see. The earlier ones, I think they made a little bit of money. But the latter ones didn't because afterwards, after the war, you know, after the World War II, well, you see the Chinese people started migrating down to California, you know. They had to call all the gardeners up there because the season is not quite that long, you see. And that's all there was, the truck gardening. So they all started moving down to California where they're more Chinese and they can have longer season and they can plant more. They can produce more. And they're a lot better down there. They can make more money with their crops then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the community united as a group on that building? I mean, was it sort of a, you know, a community, a community hall?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, the kind of community. But they rented the stores. All the families got the stores. They rented the stores to the families. Yeah, to the families. But it belongs to the way you call it. But it doesn't. The stores, because that group of families don't have that much money so they own that part of it. They can do anything they want with it. It's like a sign that says, you do so and so, you can kick out. They didn't like that. They just stay there that long until... They know that this part of it belongs to that family, that part of it belongs to that. But, you know, that is when the men themselves are young in the younger days, you know. But then as they grow older and then they get older and maybe they have their sons not so much interested in keeping up with the stores, then maybe they would sell their share to some of the other relatives or something, you know. And then gradually it kind of disintegrated because the people as they learn more English, they trade other places. They would trade Safeway probably, you know, and they get away from buying Chinese shoes then. Maybe they feel that the other slippers are just as comfortable and they can have better choice maybe. And so gradually they are buying other things, you know, rather than all Chinese things. And so it kind of scattered out a little bit more, you know, kind of disintegrated a little bit. And it's not a necessity anymore to keep these stores anymore. That's what I think it would be, you know, because the last 20, 20, 25 years or so, the building has just been rented out very cheap. Not Chinese anymore, the last 20 years or so. Well, there are not too many Americans in there, but there were a few American people there, but not too many. And then maybe some are vacant, you know, vacant and just two or three buildings are rented to a lower class business, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a meeting hall upstairs?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, they had a meeting hall upstairs. They had a meeting hall upstairs where they get once a month or so. And then they had a hall there. That meeting hall does, in case the Chinese people, you know, had a little trouble amongst themselves, and then they all get up there and discuss the thing. And then what the elders say, you're wrong, then you're wrong. That ends that one. You can't go out there and go and try to, and then tell them, I'm going to go up and appeal. No more. All the elders get together there and then he says, okay, you're wrong now, because after all, you did the wrong thing and you're wrong. And that ends it. The young one, that ends right there. That's the middle cell right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of cases would be the kinds of things that they might discuss?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, sometimes. Mostly, you know, mostly sometimes people owe some money and forget about it. Or sometimes they, you know, they might, sometimes they might have a girlfriend or something like that. Somebody might have a girlfriend or something. And then they think of the jealous types of that type, you see. But most of the time, it's usually sometimes the young kids get tempted to go into a fight because they call them a name or something like that, you know, or something of that sort. You know, it didn't in any way call them, see. But even as far as that's concerned, even the old money or anything that is concerned, if they all get together here and they talk over the case, if the way you call it, if they say that's the right thing to do, then all the way you call it get together, that's the right thing to do. And that's all there is to it. No more appeal, no more what you call it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would everybody hear, would everybody listen to the sides argue?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yeah, you can talk to both sides. You can talk to your side, you can talk to your side, and the elders sit here in the middle, all the elders, you know. The elders were the wiser ones, see. They didn't say what you call it. The elders are the one that called, you know, are the leading group of the whole thing. Chinese people as a general respect the elders because they're supposed to be the wisdom because they live longer than we do, so therefore they know what you call it. Their wisdom is what you call it. We respect them that way. So whatever they say, although sometimes maybe it's a little bit unfair, but nevertheless the elders hold them down from both sides. Supposing you were the Smith young kid and your father was, you know, your father Smith and maybe a couple of relatives, the Smiths will hold you down. So although maybe you think you're going to fight the whole bunch, but they'll only talk. Yeah, they're pretty good that way. They don't have too much trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many elders would you say there were in Walla Walla?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: One time there was 300 some Chinese people in Walla Walla. I mean elders. Well, elders, anybody's over, you know, it depends on the owner of the group. You don't have to be what you call it. Supposing everybody was, of course, Chinese people, most of them, some of them are mostly in 50s, 60s, 70s. But even if you had a group of people, you know, that are all the way from 15 or from 0 to 40, 50, it doesn't make any difference. If those 14, 15 of them get the trouble, then that settles that thing. The elders usually get the saying, the oldest one has the most to say and the most respected one. Not necessarily the oldest one, but the oldest one respected one because there are some of them older that are, of course, there are very few Chinese that are that drunk, you know, like what you call it. There are very few Chinese that do that. But the Chinese, a lot of people are quite gambler. They're pretty good gambler, some of them. But as a general rule, they don't, the most, the elders, the most successful one and the way it's called, he usually has the most to say because he is the wisest one because he's the most successful, he's the most wise one. Therefore, you have to give him respect from that point on. So that's how things are. If they get up to the point where you have to have just one guy, then that one guy would determine what is right, what is fair for both sides. They figure he is fair because he is more successful, like I say, he accomplished what you guys start not to do. So at least take the word from the wise one anyway. Just because he's old doesn't mean that he's got to be respected. He's got to raise a good, his family has to be nice people. I mean, he himself is not a gambler and a drunk and a woman and don't take care of his home. Even though he's 100 years old, he's not respected. He's got to be a person that has done well, has worked hard and has enough common sense to save and not because he's the richest but because he's maybe the wisest in the way he raises family, in the way he does business. He's the most honest, he's the one that is respected. Not these roughnecks, people that don't conform to what's right and what's wrong. We don't mean that. I wonder how he would judge, or the men would judge, the way our courts work. Now they've got so many laws, so many different ways of... Yeah, that's right. Tearing the law, bending the law that suits you. I wonder if he would judge on ethics or how he would decide who was right and who was wrong. He would listen to all the people, listen to all the testimony or what both sides had to say. No, I don't think... they argue what he called the general, the Chinese... where the family gathering like this is concerned, they don't study law that much. We have what they call ethical law that is wrong to kill, the wrong to steal, the wrong to do the Ten Commandments, all these things here. And we base upon our thoughts on those things. But supposing somebody chisels a little bit now, somebody trying to bend the law, it goes chisels a little bit on that side. Well, therefore, the elders all get together, the four or five of them, they talk about it, they consider that fellow chiseled. Okay, you chisel, therefore you are wrong. So that ends it right there. You are wrong, so you have to either straighten it out or do something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you said that you're a chiseler, then what would they do? They make them pay money?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, pay back up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they ever consider kicking somebody out of the community?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, they do consider it. If you are really what he calls, they do consider it. They all kind of ostracize you. Then you're just all alone. You have to be pretty bad to merit that. Yeah, that's right. You have to be really pretty bad. Because otherwise they've got nothing to do with it. You come in the store and nobody pays attention to you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the organization of the community in these meetings, did they have a name that they call themselves as a group?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, they have a name. I don't recall now. They have names for them. Like I said, if they have any trouble, then the people go to all those stores and they have a kind of messenger. We're going to have a meeting tonight at the Hueco discuss certain problems. They do that. Then at a certain time, everybody at a certain time can get off and go there. Okay, they all go there and talk about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do the people pay dues as well?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, the people, they don't. They pay dues to their own family or their own store. Sometimes they pay dues. Not to that. As a general rule, they have a pot of tea there you can drink. They don't have no cake or no cookies. They just have teapots. If you want to drink tea, that's fine. Chinese have the general tea. They like water. So it doesn't wait.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then it was a place that people could stay if they were coming through and traveling?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, they got a room upstairs. They got a room upstairs if you want to stay for a few days. Then they got new people that come in and want to stay and look around for a job or something.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Then they say you want to work?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Okay. Then Chinese people say so and so comes to town and they're looking for some job. Anybody can job. So the gardeners or anybody else who runs around the job, they go try and find new ones. That's another thing about this Mormon, about the beehive. In this big house, I would say 25, 30 rooms. They would have two or three rooms way up there, off to one wing. A guest room. New families that come to town looking for a job or going through. They are welcome to stay in these few rooms. I did find a job. Or at least stay during the time when they're in town. This is the way the Chinese people do it. They have nothing fancy, but at least it's out of the rain anyway. Maybe some of them have a little bit more luxury in their lives and some a little bit less. That's exactly what this is. I was mentioning about this, having a few rooms upstairs, four travelers and four people that have been out of work. Maybe sometimes for old men that have no place to go, they stay there until the relatives can take care of them better. Or friends or welfare or whatever can take care of them otherwise. This is kind of a transitional thing. That's what the small men have at this beehive house. I think in this one book that I brought along, which you might even be interested in looking at, it's called The Asian in the West.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This one guy has really done some very interesting, trying to recover some of the old ways and what it was like. In British Columbia, in the interior, they found an old... I'll show you what it was because they've got some plates. You might even be able to read some of the... ... agreement, how it would be discussed. They did a translation from the Chinese. He calls it a tang. Tang. The name of the... it was called the qigong tang. Oh, qigong hong.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Tang is actually the tong. The tong is probably Canadian. You're translating that. So your accent from one part to the other is a little different. They do have rules to set. But the main Chinese rule is do unto others as you would like to have others do unto you. That's the main... all their life is that way. But when you get out of the line that way, then if you get the hui kou ku, they are all... Like I say, the ostrich, they just won't talk to you. They usually go to places they don't want to talk to you. If you did something here, all the stores, the hui kou ku, they won't give you credit. They won't give you hui kou ku. They don't care. You're just there alone. When you get there, when they look at you, they just don't talk to you. They just ignore you. They ignore you. Then they presume you feel hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu hu. You feel exercised.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know anybody that happened to in Walla Walla?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no, no, we don't. Maybe for a little while they do that. Some young kids will get in a fight, the hui kou ku, the older folks, hui kou ku, then they jump on them. Mostly just their families do that. Then maybe they let up when he starts... Yeah, yeah, yeah, that's right, that's right, that's right. It shows up, yeah, it behaves. But you know, from reading that too, and from what you say about the rules, the regulations, I do have the idea that the people were very strong on not going to the authorities, not going to the American law. Yeah, that's right. They can't take care of themselves. They don't want to go to the American law. In the first place, they don't know English, in the first place. In another place, in order to go to the American law, you have to hire a lawyer, you have to go to certain formalities that go through, where you are in your dealing with a Chinese group, you're in your own group, therefore you've got your own law, you've got your own things there, so you go a lot faster without going through it. The American law sometimes I think is going to the other extreme, you know, just dragging your feet in. I can see why they wouldn't want to trust the American law the way it was working in those days. It was working against them, you know. Yeah, a lot of them were against them. Another thing too, Chinese people, if they lose face, that means they lose their honor. I mean, losing face means a lot to Chinese people, honor means a lot, you know, a lot more than wealth, you know, in things. So that lots of times keeps them from doing things that they might have done. Dishonored, their family. Their family especially, the old patriarch, you know, the father of the family, if you dishonor him, you know, you're really, the rest of the family look down on you too. Besides the other families look down on you too. The other families look down on your family because you have such a, such a heartable son or daughter who has done so much wrong, if he did wrong, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take much, did it take much wrong to be dishonored?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yeah, it did quite a bit. Yeah, it did quite a bit. You do have to do quite a bit. It has to be true. Yeah, it has to be quite a bit before you do. They have to make sure that it's such a thing, it's true before they, you know, discredit you. You know, supposing a little Chinese boy or whoever did steal something from the store or something like that. You know, chop a little thing from the store. Well, if the store can, if they can catch it, you tell the father, the father always pay it. The father without you, without you going through, dragging him down the court where he caught the father, always pay it because his son, although he'll take his son home and paddle his rear end, see, but he'll straighten that out. Because to us, you know, even if the kid don't know any better, well, we say that if he don't know any better, we can forgive him, you know. But if he know better and still do wrong, then he has to be punished. And we will punish him at home a lot, a lot worse than he could have been punished at the police station. At the police station, maybe they find him, you know, and let him go. Or maybe a little bit worse offense, one or two days in jail, well, how good does that do, you know. But, and then, but if he's punished at home, he's told why he's being punished and the reasoning within a lot more. Of course, the family life is very strong. Well, it's personal then and it's not just society in general. Yes, well, it's personal in the family and then your own family, individual family, and then the rest of your own lose or whatever you call it, you know, the whole family. It matters a little bit more, you know, to your own family, personal family of course, but it matters almost as much to the rest of the family too, you know. Not the personal, I mean your whole village would be, it would be a disgrace, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does this mean that there wasn't very much rebelliousness among the young or was there still rebelliousness anyway?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Not so much. No, there's very little rebelliousness among the young. Not if they are punished. That's one thing about our laws here today. One thing why we have so much crime is because the crime goes unpunished. If all the crimes are punished according to what they should be punished with, you know, like a murder, go out there and hang him or whatever, you know. If he's tried and he's found guilty, get rid of him. Why put him in jail so he can have a chance to get out in another two, three or four years, especially if they got money. Which, you know, that's not right. He killed somebody and you're taking somebody else's life, you know. But of course there's a lot of things coming into consideration. Same thing like if you're at war, we tell our young people to go out there and kill the other young people. Well, the other young people didn't do us any harm. They didn't do our young boys any harm, but yet they were told to do that. So there's a lot of into consideration about that, see. But of course that's war, you know. But then when you just go out and deliberately kill somebody because you don't like them, that's murder.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: And when you're found out and you're guilty, why do they keep them in jail?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: We have to feed them?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Why? What war?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He's no good. If he did it once, he's going to do it again. I don't care how many of these psychologists say, oh, they will change. They don't change too much. Unless it's, well, there are circumstances. Oh, there's always exceptions. There's passions, you know. At the height of your anger, maybe you do something. There is exceptions, yes. There's always exceptions. But to me, today, our law is not punishing the people that are doing the crimes. That's exactly why there is so much crime in this world. You told me before when we talked, I remember thinking about it, that in China, too, that people weren't very confident in the court system in the old days because they thought that there's a good chance they weren't going to get justice even if they deserved it, if they got to the courts. Yeah, yeah, yeah, that's right. Because in the old times, in the way, in the old days, too, in the real old days, down there, there are, you know, the world, it seems kind of funny that way, you know. We go to a point where we get down to the bottom and then we start rising and go the other way a little bit, you know, because the pendulum starts swinging the other side. When we get the other side, we have to come back. The same way in the court system, in the high court system, in the old time, when the king was real good, then everything was real, even good, see, then there was justice. But in the latter century or so, you see, the Chinese court was everybody's for money. They're like, you know, everybody's for money, and so everything goes, you give them money, they swing one way or the other. So that's one thing I respect these communists right at the present time, mainland China. I respect them from that respect because money doesn't mean anything to them. You have to go for the society and, you know, that's it. Here I just have to read in the magazine here, the Sunday paper, somebody asked, there's always the actress, actor and actress make the most money or something like that, and the reporter said that this Chinese actress in mainland China, she's a famous Chinese actress in mainland China for her 700, 800 million people, and she only gets $5 a week.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: See, here, this one here, there's somebody's thousand dollars a song, everybody's somebody's thousand dollars an actor. It sounds like from the way that the families worked and the community worked here, you know, just here, from the way that they made, that it was expected that each person would be responsible to the whole community, and wouldn't just act on his own behalf and do whatever he felt like. It sounds like that's a little similar to what the communists are doing in China. It sounds like maybe it goes back to deep feelings in Chinese people that they can work for the group instead of just for their own group. Well, you see, the Chinese for a long time was based upon a family. The family is a unit. They call it like a molecule. A molecule is a unit. They figured, you know, a Chinese figure that way. If you can raise your family and make your family, you know, run your family good, therefore you can run a village. That's the next unit, a little village. Then if you can run that good, then you can run a county. Then you can run a county, then you can run a state. If you can run that good, then you can run the government. That's why the Chinese based upon their theory is that way. But if you can manage those things and do well, then you can run the whole thing. If you can manage China well, you can run the whole world well. Because it's based upon the theory that you can do that. That sounds Confucian. Yeah, that's what Confucian's theory was. So it's based upon a family. But I don't know, sometimes I don't know, this idea. In a way, before China was bad too, then the family got so strong, they set away. The bigger family got bigger, then they fight amongst themselves. One family fights against the other family. Because in other words, the county, the state government wasn't strong enough to cover the family. But now at the present time, the central government controls the whole world. And everybody is even. So everybody has to work for one unit. One thing that this man, the way he looks at it, traces the different groups. He says, and I'm curious to know if you think this is true, he says that the first tongs, or the secret society, was the triad, he calls it the triad society. But he says that it started in China in about the 17th century. And it was a movement against the Manchu dynasty, wanting to restore the Ming dynasty. And that was the beginning of what became the society's tongs in America. When they came over here, they brought it over. Yeah, at a certain point. Because at that time, you see, those families were together back in China. When they came over here, they were all in one unit by themselves. So if you happen to be a Smith and a Jones, with friends in China, although you live in a different part of the country, therefore when you get to the United States, you want friends to help one another. So therefore you automatically go there because your tribe has been back there. So they respect you that way. You have to have that in order to survive. Otherwise you can, individually, they knock you off and you have no way of doing it. That's a survival, that's what it is.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: But then he talks about another, besides the family, besides the societies, there's another group he talks about, which he calls the Hui Quan, or I don't know how you'd say it, but it's what they sometimes call the six companies.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh look, yeah, look, what do you call it, yeah?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The Hui Quan. And he says that that was separate, that in Chinatown, in San Francisco, that that was led by the merchants. Yeah, like a chamber of commerce, something like a chamber of commerce. See, then the merchants always get together, see, and then they form it so they can import, and the merchants do it themselves in order to control the thing too, see. Otherwise, the other, the one, like I said, where you come from the tongue, I told you that Smith and Jones come together, that one doesn't do business, you see. That one is just, we are related because we are, our ancestors way back there are, what do you call it, related, so therefore we are friends, that's what he calls it. But actually, we help one another financially, what do you call it, but we don't do business, you know. We don't, I do some business for you, because there were anything like that, as a general rule we don't. Although we can get together and pot up some money, then go into business, that's fine, but it would not be, what do you call it, doing business that way. See, where the other one, six companies, those are merchants, they are like a chamber of commerce. In other words, those merchants, they have got to get together in order to, you know, what they import, otherwise one imports something else and they'll, you know, you go back to China, you buy something, then one of them sells cheaper than the other, and pretty soon they have trouble all along. So they form one company here in order to get things straight, so everybody have the same equal, you know, equality on it. It's just like a chamber of commerce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there anything like that in Walla Walla?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, nothing. We weren't in that much of a business. Yeah, there wasn't that big. We don't import too much in Walla Walla, you see. The only thing, we just get a little food for, you know, for the 300, at one time it was 300 something, but after that there wasn't that many. After this, you'd die down, die down. Oh, food and necessity. Yeah, necessity. And Chinese fish maybe, that we don't have, they have to import that. And on top of that too, you know, and they form a little bit in case somebody, you know, some gardener would like to have a, you know, buy a horse or buy a truck or buy something, you know, a wagon or something, you know, that the American people would give it to them, providing, you know, providing the store would okay it. You know, what you call, otherwise individually they wouldn't because the American people can't tell one Chinese from the other Chinese. So, so, so they wouldn't do any good. But if the store says, you, I guarantee it, it's fine. And then everything's fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Walla Walla, did most of the power, did most of the power rest with the family or did it rest with the whole organization?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The whole, the whole. The most power went to the elders mostly, to the elder people, you know, because they run the whole. But the elders made up of different members of the different families. Yeah, that's right. Maybe one or two Lu's and one or two Wong's and one or two Ang's. So then the family. Representatives, like our representatives in the House of Congress, you know. So then the family itself wasn't really as strong as the whole community was. No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. They had, they had the final say if there was a. They don't say anything unless you get into trouble. The cousins, they don't say anything. They don't buy anything. Well, the families can take their problems to the elders. And the elders, it consists of, of, of, of the whole, of all the families. Well, at first, though, I think they go to their own family first. Yeah, sure. Yeah, that's right. Within the family, then they say, just something like a small unit, your own family, and then the family of, there could be six or seven Lu families. And then maybe the elder ones from the six or seven families from the Lu's, they decide that what's best for this one individual, for this one family. Then if they can decide, then they go to the general assembly, like Washington, D .C. Same thing like, like a city and the county and then the state and then the federal, you know. Only in our way, we don't, we don't make up so that we don't vote for certain, for just individual. We vote for our own elders wanting the family to represent us because we think that they're the wisest one. And we don't, after, one another reason, too, we don't, after we got the, the, the, the, the person straightened out, we don't talk about it. One of the things is that it's there, see, we don't go out there and say, we don't go out there, we don't go out there and somebody say, oh, he, he, so, so, so, that ends it there. And, and, and, and, and. So, so the feel, if the, if the fellow, if that fellow takes it, everything is smooth, it goes, you know, without, nobody say, he did this, no, you know, you know, that feeling that, that, he's a crook where he's something like that. No, it just, that ends it there. He says he won't do it anymore, so that ends it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He did a wrong thing, and he, if the elders say he did do wrong, if he admits that he did wrong, well then that ends it, I guess, but what if he don't admit that he did wrong? Well, what if he don't admit that he did wrong?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Then, then, as I said, you get ostracized, whatever it is, then, then it's gone, that's all. And then you see other people don't give him a job, they will not let them go, go, go with their daughters, or, or, you know, they don't come to our parties, and he is not, just not invited to anything that goes on the rest of the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the whole community decide, you know, at the council, did they decide who was going to work where, on, you know, who would work on the different farms, or how was that decided?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, that, that's decided on, on between you and me. You, the employer and the employee. Yeah, yeah, you, you, you, they all get together, more or less partnership. See, if you, if you were the farmer, you know a little bit about driving a truck, okay, you be driving the truck. If I'm the farmer, I know how to plant seed, okay, I'm the seed planter. Then if somebody else know how to irrigate, you know, what you call it, okay, I'm the irrigator. See, so, then, so, that's where they work, they're together, see.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Is ... the one that sells gas here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He's out there. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you have to do something?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, he didn't get gas out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If, if we had, if there were eight guys, let's say, working on, working on one of these trucks together, did, did they all share in it equally, or did one guy, did one guy run pretty much?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They share equally. They share equally.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: So, they have one head guy?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They have, they have one guy to, to, to, to, that, that know how to plant the seed, plant things, you know, the seedings, when the plant, when the, the harvester thing. They have one guy that, that does that, but he doesn't get any more than anybody else does. He doesn't, he doesn't work shorter hours than anybody else does. So, in that respect, see, not because he, he knows, boy, he's gonna work less hours. No, he, he, he works the same hours, but his work could be different. That's all, that's all. He works the same hours, he works to be different. Then you don't know, okay, you, you, you, you, you, you go ahead and then weed the garden, the thing that you know, you go ahead and do it, see. Then you, you, you do it. And then you... Maybe, maybe, maybe the only advantage is that that guy that knows that a little bit about it, he might have to go to a, once in a while he'd go uptown to go to a meeting or something, you know, to tell them what, what, what, what it should be planted here, it should yield more, I mean, to get more or something like that. He, he, he had that advantage to go uptown and don't have to do that little work in between time. But nevertheless, he's on bins for the place. But when the, when the end year come, they divide everybody equal. Take off the boardroom, the, I mean, the, the board pay all the bills off. All the expenses. Yeah, all the expenses, everybody equal. See, that's what it is. See, and then when they buy, they buy the whole lot. Now, you know, just because he works out in the garden, he don't have to be a gardener. He can be the cook and the housekeeper, you know. Of course, there wouldn't be enough cooking for him to spend the whole day there. So, so he has to, after he gets to cooking, have it work. It depends on how, how big the farm is. If he does have to take the whole day to cook, then he don't go or work out on the farm at all, you know. But, and then he do all, just all the cooking and maybe somebody else. Yeah, somebody else, come here. Do, do some other kind of work, you know. Whatever work is divided amount equally.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, I want, I want to tell you, this is what my dehydrates. Sweet potato, I mean, yam. I just want you to try. He just, he just made it. See, I am diabetic, you know, so I'm not supposed to, but I spur it once in a while. You know, like, like anybody who has a sweet teeth, tooth. So he dehydrates these things. No, no sugar, but just dehydrate it. And I chew on those instead of a chew on a piece of candy or a cookie, see. That's good. That's right. Oh, these are almond cookies I got from Los Angeles. Very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hi, how are you?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: How come chow, chow go outside? Come on, chow.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Do you like that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think this is a sweet potato, that's a yam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know why one is, is this a sweet potato, one's a sweet potato and the other a yam?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That's a sweet potato and that's a yam. Yeah. I just imagined what he'd call it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About the truck gardening, was the rent that they had to pay on the land, was it high? The amount of money they had to pay?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, of course, it depends on the landlord, you know. Some landlords were, at that time, no Chinese, but some of the American people up in their age, homesteaded, a lot of those at that time, see. And they were willing to rent it to them fairly cheap because they hired Chinese here for a long time and otherwise they'd plant pasture or a little grass or a little wheat or something and a little bit, it doesn't pay them, see. Most of those farmers are, they got about 40 acres or something, most of those small, real homestead, they got about 40 acres, see. So when they plant wheat, they don't get too much for it because they're too small a chunk to cut, although they have a little hay. But the Chinese, as a general rule, the Chinese people pay them a little more than they get out of hay. So I think at that time they pay $35 to $45 an acre, you see, for a year, see. That's what they usually pay. Some of them, 50, afterwards they got a little more than that, see. But that's the general pay, but that's pretty good, you know, at that time. You can buy land for about $100, $150 an acre, you know, at that time.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Less than that at that time, isn't it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, I bet. Herst, grandfather, we saw Herst's Castle down, of course this is the third time we saw it, 50 cents an acre. But then the guy's saying, well, don't try to buy it now, you know, for that much because you won't get it. But rent is 285 ,000 acres, Herst's. So people could afford to rent the land. Well, they have to rent the land, you see, but the Chinese can't own it. Because we can't buy it. Yeah, you can't buy it, you see, so they have to rent it. Otherwise Chinese will own most of that land, you see. So if they have to just rent it, that's all they can do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they have a hard time finding people who would rent it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yeah, they don't have too much hard trouble that way. Because as a general rule, the Chinese will keep the weeds out and keep the things in there, you know, pretty good that way, see, so they don't have too much trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any particular kind of land that they looked for to park? Was there land they preferred to park?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yeah, sure, they would like to have, you know, agriculture truck garden land. You had to have the soil, fairly good soil, you know, level, because they have to irrigate it. You know, you can't have it on the hillside or anything like that, you see. So they tried to, at that time, you see, the town wasn't built out too far, you know, there are lots of those, but now they've got all the lots, you see, so that's how it is. Otherwise, they didn't have too much. Like I said, one time there were 25 gardens all around that territory. Now there's houses all over the place now. Now there's only one hardware. Yeah, see, I mean the houses, the people, you know, the Italians buy five acres, six acres, and they build a home there, and then they go and plant a little new bar with a little onions and a little asparagus on it, see. And they kind of make a little living on it, and that's all, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they irrigate then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Water, they did, the water river.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how did they, did they just dig trenches in it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, they have big trenches, yeah. They have no sprinkles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the modern day today, they use the sprinkles?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yeah, modern day. Oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Tomcat?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah, modern day, but they used to, that's all, they used to be, when you have a garden, you've got to have a spring, you've got to have water to run through the garden, run through the land, see. So they put a little dam there, then they run into a ditch, and then they go ahead and...

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Because Ed, you've been using sprinkles for about, what, 40 years now, hasn't it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I don't think it's that long, I guess, it's in the last way. At, oh, at least 30 years.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You know, you finished school about 30 years ago?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, I have finished school, I got here. And of course, you know, in the old days, you had to get up in the middle of the night to go and move your horses too, you know. So he had to do that, I know for years he had to do that. This is the last family down there now that owns a truck garden farm. They themselves have 50 acres at the most, the original one's 40, and then they had about 10 more, and then they are still renting some too. Every year they rent, they go into sugar beets now, sugar beets and spinach and Chinese vegetables.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: My, what would you guess, at the end of the year, and after it was all divided up and all the costs were paid, including the rent on the land, how much do you think a guy would make?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, that depends from year to year, you see. That's just like a farmer, you know, a farmer is a life of what he calls. Some, I've been in the raising garden quite a long time, and I know some years, some good years, they make $1 ,200. And some years, they're not so good, they come out only maybe $100, $150. But they very seldom, you hear them lose, because they always make enough to pay the food, the board bill, and the land, and the rent. But outside that, they get nothing for themselves when they hear it, I've seen them now. I myself had, when the Chinese gave me a garden, because it wasn't doing any good, I myself, yeah, the price wasn't good. I sold onions for 25 cents a sack, 100 pounds. I sold them myself. I was only a high school boy at that time, and the onion market was glutted, you know, and those produce houses.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So I was, so I went to, I went to Walla, I went to Portland, I went to Portland myself. I was just a high school kid then, I went to Portland myself, and I sold six carloads of onions myself. And I only sold them for 25 cents a sack, see. Then of course the Chinese people were, you know, I was a boy, what do you call it, and they sold it to me for 15 cents a sack. See, because I have to have a little bit in order to do what you call it, see. To go truck it to Portland, from Walla Walla to Portland. Then, then, then, then, then, you know, then, like I said, I was the one that sold six cars, but that six cars, six cars, you see, at that time it was 100 pound sack. And one car is about 350, 350 sacks to a car, a carload, see. I had to go and, and, and, and load the cars. See, I had to go load the cars, see, because that's, that's my commission off of it, see. And, and I had to go and get the money back from the produce houses, you see, to pay them off, see. And of course, like I said, they always trusted me, so they all bring the onions up to me, and I loaded, I had to pay, help, another boy helped me, so we'd load the, excuse me, I got to load the onions in the car.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The first time I went to Portland, you know, do any kind of business that way, see, and of course I was in high school at that time, I think it was either first or second year, and I was in, you know, so I just went to Portland. That's in about 1923 then, because 1925 you came to college. That sounds like a lot of responsibility. It is, for a little, for boys, I know when I got the check for that, for that much money, I think it must be, let's see, I forgot how much it was, a little boy, you know, a little high school kid that got a check on it, and then went to the bank, and then, and then tried to get the cash, and I could pay these people off, they had, they looked at me, and then they asked me who I was, and then I had to go, and had had my father, and you know, had some of the older Chinese that knows me, that say it was okay before they would cash it, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it, do you remember the trip to Portland, I mean when you were in Portland, what you did?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It sounds like a, pretty much an adventure. Well, there was one, one of the Chinese boys that haulsed up to Portland, he haulsed up to Portland, I mean, he haulsed up to Portland anyway, see, so I got a ride with him, you see, he wanted to sell too, so I went along with him, see, I went along with him, and then of course I went down there, I look up the produce dealers, the Pacific Fruit, and then at that time there was two, three other, Rhine Fruit, and all the other stuff that handled, sell these fruit and produce, see, so I went and told him, because I told him I'll sell him the onion for 25 cents FOB, loaded right on the wall, to them, see, it was actually so cheap, but my guys, they couldn't, they say okay, so each one of them took a few cars, so I went down, after I got home, you see, and then I had to go call the railroad company up to get the car at a certain siding, so I can load there, you see, and then on top of that I have to load and have to stack them a 5 -6 high, you know, in order to get the 300s. And that's 25 cents for 100 pound, they don't sell 100 pound anymore, these are mostly 50 pounds today, 50 pound the other day was $34, you know, I mean not very long ago, and so, You went, did you go the first time to go to Portland, did you go by train with this, No, no, with a little truck, with a truck, yeah, a little truck, you know, go down there, yeah.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: But you, you load the onion on the train though, huh?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yeah, on cars, cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to go with the onions to Portland?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, just as soon as I loaded on and then see that I took it to the railroad company, you know, tell the oil call down there, certain cars at certain siding, and then all down to Portland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in Portland, did you stay with relatives?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, I just stayed one day, once they went down there and came back, because just as soon as you get the order, you see, those guys want it right now, see, In order to do that, I have to make, at that time you can't go to Portland in five hours like I do now, see, And it takes you all day to go to Portland, see, just as soon as they come back, they say, I have a certain oil call, so I have to do it right away, And you gotta give these people time to cut their onions, to load the sack there, and then too, you know, It's a little hard to do. That's what if I did the sack, and don't forget, it cost you so much for the bag too, you know, and it cost you so much to raise the onion, cost you so much to cut them, And then bag them, and get it down to FOB, you say, from Walla Walla, see, as soon as he load them onto the train at the siding at Walla Walla, then his job is done. That's 25 cents FOB then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you make from that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, I made, let's see, about, oh I forgot now what I made, I think I made about 150, around $150, $175, something like that. You see, I have to pay somebody for, to help me, to help me load and all that other stuff too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it your idea, or was it your mother's?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, no, it was my idea, it was my idea, you know, I had to figure out some way to get rid of it, Which I didn't want it, I usually go and haul cabbage up and give it to the vegetable, grocery store, you know, the private grocery store. You see, the produce houses, they gotta make a certain percentage otherwise they won't handle the talk, see, But I can, as a grower, I can cut it and give it to them and make them sell the same price as it would to the producer, And the producer could, I mean, the retailer could sell it and make a little bit of money that the producer would otherwise could get, see. So that's why I say that the government is controlling these things, they're not good, see. They should let the producer push it out the salad, then the thing would drop, see, because this way here, the commission house, they gotta make something, Then the ship at the Seattle, Spokane, Spokane houses, truck it down here, here, something else, you see. Then the price goes up so high that it's hard to do. Maybe you get a little bit better product sometimes, but not always, see. But this way here, the farmer, he raises it, okay, he sells it, then you have to eliminate maybe two middlemen. You probably eliminate two middlemen. I think at the time when you were thinking about selling this onion too, you know, you're not only thinking of making a little money, But he see all the onions there, you know, on the field, no market for it, so he thought that he would do them a little favor too in order to get them, And so the farmer wanted to give him a little commission, so they sell to him for fifteen cents a stack. You know, onions are quite a hard thing to do, you know. If you don't get rid of it, they sprout on you, see. You plow it under you, they still sprout on you, see. You have more trouble, see. You have to scoop them all in one pile and chop them all up or something, because they're not like the thing you just plowed over in lettuce or something. Cabbage you plowed over in that end, see, because it won't sprout. But onion you plowed over next spring, you cut them all over the place. It's quite a thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They gave you, you got a plot to garden yourself that year?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, no. You said you were gardening too. Oh, I worked in the garden all the time, you know, every summer. He worked for them after school, he'd ride a bicycle out there too. Yeah, I worked for them every summer. He's out there too, see. It sounds to me from what I have, you know, the little I know about gardening and about hand gardening, it'd be pretty hard work. It is. It's hard work. It's back aching work. You have to crawl on your hands and knees, then weed it, then on top of that you see that sometimes your hands and knees, you got rock, you know, a little rock hit your knee on the boy, that funny bone on your knee. It's in the job that the American people like, you know. That's why only the Italians, what do you call it, do these kind of work. But one summer though, you know, the onion crop was so bad, that was when he was in college, the price was so bad for the onion, the onion crop was good. But the price was so bad that one of the gardeners said, you take my field of onion, do what you can with it. So he made a little money that summer too. That's the only time, you know. I never heard about this one, that you load the onion. I remember he told me about the other one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Even when you were in college, you were working the summer? Did you still work in the summer?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yeah, I go back and work. I have to work a little bit at first, my own spending money, you know. His father gave him tuition. He has to work for his own... Spending money, clothes and stuff that way, you see. While he was going to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do when you worked in the garden? Was it the waiting most? For everything?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Everything, just like anything else, whatever you can. Of course I'm the huskiest one. So therefore I did most of the loading. You know, the way you call it, lifting all the... He did the lifting. Yeah, I'm the huskiest one. So I load most of the time. It depends on what you're able to do the best for them. You don't expect an older man, 50, 60 years old, to go ahead and load all these onions because they break the poor guy's back. So therefore when you're big, you're husking, you do the... What you can do the best, you know, for them, see.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The 12, 14 hours a day you've worked on. Yeah, yeah. You know, the 12, 14 hours. Yes, you do. More than 12 hours. I tell you, you get up about 5 .30, you get up at 5 o 'clock, then you wash your face, then you eat breakfast, then you go to work about 6 o 'clock in the morning. You go to work about 6 o 'clock in the morning at that time, 6 o 'clock in the summertime, the sun's up pretty good, see. So you go to work there, then you go working about 10 o 'clock, then you have a little, what do you call it, maybe 15 minutes or so. They bring you out a little bread and a few cookies, or maybe if the bosses are good they buy you a doughnut or so, then with a little doughnut and tea. If not, you just get a little bread. Or a cracker, or you get crackers or so, see. Then after that you go to 12 o 'clock at noon, then you eat. You go back to that farmhouse. You go back to the farmhouse and eat. The farmhouse. Yeah, where the people work, you know, to cook. Where they sleep. It's a little farmhouse where all the men that works in there, they live there, you know, they don't go back to town. No, they don't go back. No, no, no. Some of them are about 4, 3, 4 miles out and they all... And they don't have any family. They're just married men, but they don't have any family. They all live in one house on the field. Oh, and... They're like a bunkhouse, you know, they're like these migrants or whatever. They're very comfortable with our modern days. Yeah, one house. Our house too. They don't have any running water in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of beds?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, boards. And then they hand pump water. I saw that. Boards are not like the mattress. No, that's it boards. Most importantly, if you are a weight -caught, sometimes you can't do it. One of the reasons you don't have mattresses is too much because you've got bugs on them, see, out there. So you better have just a board and a weight -caught. You know, your floor is dirt, you know, your dirt floor, you see. So you have insects in the things that way and it crawls around a little bit, see. But when you have a board, it doesn't make a difference. You get it all off of you. It's very primitive. Very primitive. Then, so you go in and the lunch is at 12. Lunch is at 12. Then at 1 o 'clock, you'll rest from 12 to 1. You eat and rest. You rest maybe half an hour. If you eat fast, you'll rest a little bit longer. If you eat slow, you'll last about 15 minutes or so. And you get one hour off. Yeah, you get one hour off. Then one o 'clock, then you work until 8 o 'clock at nighttime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Straight?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, and then you get about 4 o 'clock, you get off tea again. You get your tea and your donut or your tea. Or watermelon or something like that. If the boss is, if that year hasn't been a good year, maybe he'll split it and give you a piece of cake. Yeah, buy cake or buy a ham. Buy a ham, make sandwiches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you said the boss, who do you mean the boss?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You know, if... The head guy. I mean, the group, you know, there's always, there's one person that goes out and sells things. He considered that because he speaks English, he goes out and takes orders. You know, supposing he wants so many hundred sacks of onions a day, well, he had to go take orders for it. So he buys the groceries. So he buys the groceries and buys the things home. If the prices are good, the way he calls it, then he brings home something, you know, a little exceptional, the way he calls it. Of course that's taking him out of the whole pot. Yeah, out of the whole group, but nobody minds it because everybody's having to share. Yeah, everybody gets a share of it. So they don't mind. But he takes it out of the pot. He takes it out of the pot to buy whatever grocery he needs. So it's six in the morning to eight at night. Night time, yeah. At least. At least. Until I put it in the dark. And if somebody wants an order in a hurry, you have to work that much longer in order to get that order out. Or if your vegetable happens to have to be cut right now, you know, by tomorrow, it's not so good. You cut it at the best condition. You work longer today and get it out into the warehouse or to the produce man so you can get the best price for your produce.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And then you have to work harder. But you always have to work until, oh, some eight o 'clock anyway at night, until you can't see anymore outside. And then you go back to the house and kerosene lamps. And then you see, of course, he doesn't stay out there because he has a place to stay with his father in one room. Well, I have to go. I stay out there in the summer. Oh, in the summer when you do too. I never knew that. I thought you always go back to Walla Walla.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Of course, that's only about five or six miles, isn't it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, that's a long time to run around. After you were able to go 14 hours a day, you would run a bicycle 14 days. When you were in grade school, you never had a bicycle either. That's a long time. That's a long ways off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then when the harvest was over, did the men go back into Walla Walla for the winter?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no. They do. Some of them go. When the thing's over in the wintertime, there isn't too much to do. Then some men stay in Walla Walla overnight. But as a general, they go. They go back to the place. Because during the winter months, they have to clean the irrigation ditches and things that way. The things they have to do. Their fields have been washed at certain points. They have to cut all the weeds and things. So when the springtime comes, you think everything will run smooth again. Because once the vegetables get going, you haven't got time to clean the ditches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this year round, you haven't got time to clean the ditches?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, but it's not so hectic in the winter. You can take a day off and straighten out your fare. Or maybe sometimes they can even go off for one or two days. Oh yeah, you can. I suppose that the men would want to go into town once in a while. Every weekend, they go for... every Saturday, they get off at five o 'clock. Instead of eight o 'clock, they get off at five o 'clock. Then they go to town and they stay until about eleven. Then they come on home.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: And then they all get into one taxi?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, one truck or one taxi. Sometimes they have to truck themselves. And they all get in there and they all come back every Saturday. Then when the circus comes to town, they get a day off. And when it rains, they get a day off. So those are the days... But it only rains so hard that they can work outdoors and they get a day off. You can just sprinkle and if possibly they can work outside, they still work. When the circus comes, then they get a day off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they went to town on Saturday, what would they do? Maybe gamble?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, gamble a little bit. A lot of them gamble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they stop in the stores then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, some of them go to show and some of them go to the stores. Some of them gamble a little bit. And probably they go to this... They usually have a room or probably kind of a gathering room. Some of the other friends from the other farms they come into. And chew the fat, you know, like maybe they play... March on, they play march on and they play cards, play everything. They could go into one of the rooms at the store. Whichever store has the most room, probably that's the place where they usually gather. Especially if the man is pretty nice about letting them use the room. Or maybe have a pot of tea sitting around and have a few teacups that are clean. They can do that or something like that. It's kind of a community affair and kind of get together social. That's all the social life they have all year long. Maybe on Chinese New Year though. Oh yeah, they get two weeks off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people do you think, just as a guess, how many people live in town as compared to those that lived on the farms, in the farmhouse?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I would say about 15 percent maybe lived in town. So about 85 percent aren't farmers and truck drivers. And the ones that live in town... Out of the 300 white cars, there's only two of them. Two women that have families. All the rest of them are just bachelors. See, they weren't allowed to have their wives come over. They're your restrictions again. And it's very hard. You have to be a businessman and lots of string tied to it. You have to unravel that. It takes years in order to get your wife over. I read that the wives who were here were more or less secluded in the early days. They very rarely went, at least on China downstairs, they very rarely went out... They don't go out to buy groceries. The husbands go out to buy the groceries. They stay at home. Because they don't speak English in the first place. That's why they're secluded. You can't, when you go to someplace, strange land, you don't know how to speak the way. Therefore, yes, they all live. It must have been a very lonely life for them. It is. In America with no other women to have a friend. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, just like the pioneer women, how many women are together in the pioneer days?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You live ten miles away from each other, you don't get to see each other maybe four or five times a year. That's right. You know, because it's hard to get around to see each other. Well, in this what I was reading, a couple of different writers say that was the hardest, you know, the hardest and the biggest part of the problem was that it was very unnatural to have to deny to not be able to have the families and wives. Yes, it is. That's right, that's right. Especially where the Chinese family is the main thing. The family, Chinese is the unit. It breaks it. That's one reason too why all these wives that are left back in China, of these 300 men that are there, I imagine more than half of them are married, you know, left their wives there. They don't go and remarry back there. They don't go divorce their husbands. The ones that are lucky are the ones that, like this top garden, if they happen to have a few good years, they save their money, don't spend it, you know, then they're lucky to be able to get back to China and stay one or two months with their family and their wives. See, some of the ones that don't make too much money, they don't get back at all to save their wives. We've known many, many newly married bridegrooms that come over here. When they come over here, they say, oh, we're going to try to work hard, but sometimes circumstances that they cannot save enough to go back. If they go back, they have to have enough money to live on and maybe a little bit of luxury because what you used to go back, they would be a detriment to their family back there, you know, because there would be no more money for them to live. As long as they're here, they send a little money back there, the family can live back there. But if they themselves go back there, that means their income is cut off here, then they have to live on what they save or what their wife had saved during the time when they did have a little money.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So many, many of these married men don't even get a chance to go back at all. It's only that, well, I think more than half, at least two -thirds of the husbands do get to go back. It's just a scarce case that the husbands don't get to go back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often do you think they went back and stayed permanently instead of coming back here again?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They don't go back there permanently until they can't work over here anymore. They like to go back to die. They like the salmon, you know. That's all. Because as long as they can work, they can work over here and be frugal and saving, and then the more they... If they happen to be very good at business and the business has been good to them, have been good years, then they can go back in 20 years, 30 years maybe and stay there, see. But usually when they cannot work anymore over here before they go back permanently to stay. But when they are young and left their young wives back there, then they usually like to go back as often as they can. But usually these oftentimes turn out to be six or seven years in between. That's why in my own family, see, I was born after my father came over here. They were married four years or whatever. I was born after my father came here. And then my sister is six years younger. So you can imagine how long my father was over here before he went back. At least five and a half years I would say to her because it takes nine months for a child. Well, my sister was six years younger. And then my father came back over here again. And then we were lucky to be able, he was able to send for us then. We came over in 1920. And so my third sister was born, she was 13 years younger than I am. She was born 1923. So we came over in 1921, the Christmas of 1920, which is almost 1921. So she was born two years after we came over here. So you can tell how often the men go back by their children's ages almost. You know, unless they stay home, you know, that's naturally, they have children.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Do you remember at all about how much it cost for a man to go back over and come back over here again for his passing both ways?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Mi can tell you more about that. He went back in 1929.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: How much did it cost you Mi? $125 one way?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: One way. Yeah, you go on a boat and go on about 150. On a boat. On a boat.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: One way?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: One way. From Seattle. It takes 23 days though. Yeah, 23 days. On a freighter, kind of a freighter.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, he went back, you went back on a freighter?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I thought... Long, you know, a little fun on the line and all those people. They're all freight and two packers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the men go back in groups or just a single by themselves?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
